missed.description: Phase I reactions (oxidation, reduction, hydrolysis) expose or introduce functional groups, preparing the molecule for conjugation; these may produce active or toxic metabolites

Phase II reactions are conjugation reactions (most commonly glucuronidation by UDP-glucuronosyltransferase); they attach polar groups producing highly water-soluble, usually inactive metabolites for renal/biliary excretion

CYP3A4 is the most abundant hepatic CYP isoenzyme (~50% of all drug metabolism); it metabolizes fentanyl, sufentanil, alfentanil, and midazolam; inhibitors (ketoconazole, grapefruit juice) and inducers (rifampin) dramatically alter clearance

Remifentanil contains a methyl ester linkage hydrolyzed by nonspecific tissue and plasma esterases (not pseudocholinesterase, not CYP450) — this organ-independent metabolism gives it a CSHT of ~3-5 minutes regardless of infusion duration or hepatic/renal function
Halothane hepatitis occurs via reductive (anaerobic) Phase I metabolism producing reactive trifluoroacetyl chloride intermediates that trigger immune-mediated hepatocyte destruction
Codeine is a prodrug requiring CYP2D6 O-demethylation to morphine for analgesic effect; poor metabolizers get no relief, ultrarapid metabolizers may produce dangerous morphine levels

errors.statement: It gets cleared by the kidneys faster than fentanyl
errors.correction: Remifentanil is metabolized by plasma and tissue esterases, not cleared by kidneys

errors.statement: Phase I and Phase II both happen in the liver using CYP enzymes
errors.correction: Phase II involves conjugation reactions (like glucuronidation), not CYP enzymes
Remifentanil is metabolized by nonspecific esterases, not CYP3A4

summary: Student demonstrated no understanding of drug metabolism pathways or remifentanil's unique esterase-based clearance mechanism. Multiple fundamental errors regarding CYP enzymes and renal clearance require immediate remediation.
voss_quip: Did you confuse remifentanil with furosemide? Back to basics.
